attributes.name: Windows Defender Tamper Protection Disabled via Registry
attributes.description: Detects modification of the Windows Defender TamperProtection registry value to 0, which disables anti-tampering protections and allows further security tool modifications. This is a critical indicator of T1562.001 (Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools) used by threats like CyberEye RAT.
attributes.query:
registry where event.type == "change" and
  registry.path : "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Features\\TamperProtection" and
  registry.data.strings == "0" and
  not process.executable : ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe", "C:\\Program Files\\Windows Defender\\MsMpEng.exe", "C:\\ProgramData\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Platform\\*\\MsMpEng.exe")
false_positives: Legitimate endpoint management tools modifying Defender settings via MDM or SCCM
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
attributes.note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating Tamper Protection Disabled

1. Identify the process that modified the registry value.
2. Check if the change was initiated by a legitimate management tool (Intune, SCCM).
3. Look for associated PowerShell execution with `-ExecutionPolicy Bypass`.
4. Check for subsequent registry modifications to other Defender keys.
5. Verify current Defender operational status on the host.

### False Positive Analysis

- MDM solutions (Microsoft Intune) may modify this value during policy enforcement.
- Windows Update processes may temporarily modify Defender settings.

### Response Actions

- Immediately verify Defender status: `Get-MpComputerStatus`
- Isolate the host if modification was unauthorized.
- Restore TamperProtection to 1 and investigate the attack chain.

attributes.name: Windows Defender AntiSpyware Disabled via Policy Registry
attributes.description: Detects creation or modification of the DisableAntiSpyware registry value under the Windows Defender policy key. Setting this value to 1 effectively disables Windows Defender entirely. This technique is used by CyberEye RAT and numerous other malware families.
attributes.query:
registry where event.type in ("creation", "change") and
  registry.path : "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\DisableAntiSpyware" and
  registry.data.strings == "1"
false_positives: Third-party antivirus installations that legitimately disable Windows Defender
Group Policy changes by administrators during planned maintenance
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
attributes.note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating AntiSpyware Disabled

1. Determine if a third-party AV was intentionally installed (which disables Defender).
2. Check Group Policy Objects for legitimate DisableAntiSpyware settings.
3. Correlate with PowerShell script block logs for the execution context.
4. Look for the parent process that initiated the registry change.

attributes.name: Windows Defender Real-Time Protection Components Disabled
attributes.description: Detects modification of Real-Time Protection registry values under Windows Defender policy keys. Attackers disable behavior monitoring, on-access protection, and real-time scanning to evade detection. Multiple modifications in sequence are a strong indicator of automated defense evasion.
attributes.query:
sequence by host.id with maxspan=60s
  [registry where event.type in ("creation", "change") and
    registry.path : "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableBehaviorMonitoring" and
    registry.data.strings == "1"]
  [registry where event.type in ("creation", "change") and
    registry.path : "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableOnAccessProtection" and
    registry.data.strings == "1"]
  [registry where event.type in ("creation", "change") and
    registry.path : "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableScanOnRealtimeEnable" and
    registry.data.strings == "1"]
false_positives: Bulk Group Policy application during domain join or policy refresh
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
attributes.note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating Real-Time Protection Disabled

This rule uses EQL sequence detection to identify three related registry modifications within 60 seconds, which is characteristic of automated tooling rather than manual changes.

1. Identify the source process for all three modifications.
2. Check if the process is PowerShell executing a script.
3. Look for TamperProtection and DisableAntiSpyware modifications in the same timeframe.
4. Escalate immediately if all three RTP components were disabled.

attributes.name: PowerShell Execution Policy Bypass Targeting Defender Registry Keys
attributes.description: Detects PowerShell processes launched with ExecutionPolicy Bypass that subsequently access Windows Defender registry keys. This pattern matches the CyberEye RAT attack chain where a dropped script is executed with bypass parameters to modify Defender settings.
attributes.query:
sequence by host.id, user.name with maxspan=30s
  [process where event.type == "start" and
    process.name : "powershell.exe" and
    process.args : "-ExecutionPolicy" and
    process.args : "Bypass" and
    process.args : "-File"]
  [registry where event.type in ("creation", "change") and
    registry.path : ("HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\*",
                     "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\*")]
false_positives: Administrative scripts that legitimately query or configure Defender settings
Endpoint management tools that use PowerShell for Defender configuration
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
attributes.note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating PowerShell Bypass with Defender Registry Access

This is a sequence rule that correlates PowerShell execution (with bypass flags) followed by Defender registry modifications.

1. Examine the full PowerShell command line for the script path and arguments.
2. Retrieve the script content from Script Block Logging (Event ID 4104).
3. Analyze the script for Defender-disabling intent.
4. Check if the script was dropped recently (file creation time).
5. Investigate the parent process of PowerShell for initial access vector.

attributes.name: Complete Windows Defender Disable Sequence Detected
attributes.description: Detects the full CyberEye-style attack sequence: PowerShell execution with bypass parameters followed by TamperProtection disable and AntiSpyware policy disable within 120 seconds. This high-confidence rule identifies the complete defense evasion kill chain.
attributes.query:
sequence by host.id with maxspan=120s
  [process where event.type == "start" and
    process.name : "powershell.exe" and
    process.args : "-ExecutionPolicy" and
    process.args : "Bypass"]
  [registry where event.type in ("creation", "change") and
    registry.path : "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Features\\TamperProtection" and
    registry.data.strings == "0"]
  [registry where event.type in ("creation", "change") and
    registry.path : "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\DisableAntiSpyware" and
    registry.data.strings == "1"]
false_positives: Extremely unlikely in legitimate operations. This sequence should always be investigated.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
attributes.note:
## Triage and Analysis

### CRITICAL - Full Defense Evasion Kill Chain

This rule triggers only when the complete attack sequence is observed. False positives are extremely unlikely.

### Immediate Actions

1. **ISOLATE** the affected host immediately.
2. Capture volatile evidence (running processes, network connections).
3. Verify Defender operational status.
4. Check for subsequent malicious activity that leveraged the disabled defenses.
5. Restore Defender settings and investigate full attack timeline.

### Response Priority: P1 - Critical

attributes.name: PowerShell Script Block Logging - Defender Disabling Commands
attributes.description: Detects PowerShell script block log entries containing commands that modify Windows Defender registry keys to disable protection components. This covers both Set-ItemProperty and direct reg.exe commands targeting Defender configuration.
attributes.query:
any where event.provider == "Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell" and
  event.code == "4104" and
  message : ("*Set-ItemProperty*Windows Defender*TamperProtection*",
             "*Set-ItemProperty*Windows Defender*DisableAntiSpyware*",
             "*Set-ItemProperty*Windows Defender*DisableBehaviorMonitoring*",
             "*Set-ItemProperty*Windows Defender*DisableOnAccessProtection*",
             "*Set-ItemProperty*Windows Defender*DisableScanOnRealtimeEnable*",
             "*Set-MpPreference*-DisableRealtimeMonitoring*$true*",
             "*Set-MpPreference*-DisableBehaviorMonitoring*$true*",
             "*Set-MpPreference*-DisableIOAVProtection*$true*")
false_positives: Legitimate administrative scripts that configure Defender settings during deployment
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
attributes.note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating PowerShell Defender Disabling Commands

This rule leverages PowerShell Script Block Logging (Event ID 4104) to detect the actual script content being executed, regardless of obfuscation at the command line level.

1. Review the full script block content from the event.
2. Identify the script source (file path, inline command, or encoded).
3. Check for additional suspicious commands in the same script block.
4. Correlate with process creation events for the PowerShell session.

attributes.name: Linux Security Tool or Service Disabled
attributes.description: Detects attempts to disable Linux security tools such as SELinux, AppArmor, auditd, or endpoint protection agents. This is the Linux equivalent of T1562.001 where attackers disable host-based defenses before conducting further malicious activity.
attributes.query:
process where event.type == "start" and (
  (process.name == "setenforce" and process.args == "0") or
  (process.name == "systemctl" and process.args in ("stop", "disable") and
    process.args : ("apparmor*", "auditd*", "firewalld*", "iptables*", "nftables*",
                    "falcon-sensor*", "cbagentd*", "qualys*", "ossec*", "wazuh*",
                    "crowdstrike*", "mdatp*", "elastic-agent*", "filebeat*")) or
  (process.name == "service" and process.args == "stop" and
    process.args : ("apparmor", "auditd", "firewalld", "iptables",
                    "falcon-sensor", "cbagentd", "qualys-cloud-agent", "ossec", "wazuh-agent")) or
  (process.name == "aa-teardown") or
  (process.name == "sysctl" and process.args : "kernel.yama.ptrace_scope=0") or
  (process.name : ("chkconfig", "update-rc.d") and process.args : ("auditd", "apparmor", "firewalld") and process.args == "off")
)
false_positives: Automated deployment scripts during system provisioning
Kernel upgrades that temporarily stop auditd
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
attributes.note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating Linux Security Tool Disabled

1. Identify the user account that executed the command.
2. Check if the action was part of a planned change window.
3. Look for subsequent suspicious activity (file drops, lateral movement).
4. Verify current security tool status on the host.
5. Check if the service was re-enabled after the event.

attributes.name: macOS Security Protection Disabled
attributes.description: Detects attempts to disable macOS security protections including Gatekeeper, XProtect, and endpoint security agents. This is the macOS equivalent of T1562.001 where attackers disable platform security controls.
attributes.query:
process where event.type == "start" and (
  (process.name == "spctl" and process.args : ("--master-disable", "--disable")) or
  (process.name == "defaults" and process.args == "write" and
    process.args : ("com.apple.LaunchServices", "com.apple.XProtect*") and
    process.args : ("LSQuarantine", "XProtect*")) or
  (process.name == "launchctl" and process.args in ("unload", "disable") and
    process.args : ("*com.apple.XProtect*", "*com.apple.MRT*", "*com.apple.security*",
                    "*com.crowdstrike*", "*com.carbonblack*", "*com.microsoft.wdav*")) or
  (process.name == "csrutil" and process.args == "disable") or
  (process.name == "mdatp" and process.args : ("--exclusion", "real-time-protection") and process.args : ("add", "disable")) or
  (process.name == "xattr" and process.args == "-cr" and process.args : "/Applications/*")
)
false_positives: Developers disabling Gatekeeper for unsigned development builds
IT administrators deploying software that requires temporary Gatekeeper bypass
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
attributes.note:
## Triage and Analysis

### Investigating macOS Security Protection Disabled

1. Identify the user and process that initiated the security change.
2. Determine if the action is part of approved software installation.
3. Check if Gatekeeper/XProtect was re-enabled after the event.
4. Look for subsequent unsigned binary execution.
5. Verify SIP status with `csrutil status`.
